 calculation of mass of carbon (1)  calculation of mass of hydrogen and oxygen (1)  calculation of moles of carbon, hydrogen and oxygen (1)  calculation of ratio (1) alternative method M1 moles of Q = 4.91 ÷  148 =  0.0332 (mol) M2 moles of CO = 14.6 ÷  44 = 0.332 (mol) and moles of H O = 3.58 ÷  18 = 0.199 (mol) M3 calculation of the ratio 10 : 12 M4 show that O = 1 (10 x 12) + (12 x 1) + (16 x number of oxygen atoms) = 148 , so O = 1 Example of calculation C H O mass = 14.6 x (12 ÷ 44) = 3.982 (g) 3.58 x (2 ÷ 18) = 0.398 (g) 4.91 –(3.982+0.398) =0.53 (g) Moles = = 3.982 ÷ = 0.332 (mol) = 0.398 ÷ = 0.398 (mol) 0.53 ÷ 0.0331 (mol) Ratio 10 Formula = C H O Allow TE from M1 to M3 (4) Question Number Acceptable Answers Additional Guidance Mark

An explanation that makes reference to the following points: structure  correct structure (1) chemical shifts - allow any three from four  4 peaks / chemical shifts means 4 (different) hydrogen environments (1)  peak at 7.5 ppm is due (hydrogen atoms on) benzene ring (1)  peak at 2.3 ppm is due H -C-C=O / ketone / C=O (1)  peak at 1.0 ppm is due to H -C-C / alkyl group / methyl group / alkane (1) splitting patterns - allow any two from three  peak at 2.3 ppm is a quartet as there are 3 hydrogens on neighbouring carbon / it is bonded to a CH group (1) Or  peak at 1.0 ppm is a triplet as there are 2 hydrogens on neighbouring carbon / bonded to a CH (1) Or  peak at 3.6 ppm is a singlet as there are no hydrogens on neighbouring carbon (1) area under curve  (area of 5 for peak at 7.5 ppm) shows the benzene ring has (only) 1 side group / is C H (1) Allow chemical environment Ignore references to peak at 3.6 ppm Do not award aldehyde If no reference to neighbouring hydrogens in M4, M5 or M6 allow 1 mark for idea that a quartet-triplet pattern is due to CH CH Ignore references to splitting of peak at 7.5 ppm Allow benzene has 5 H (atoms) DNA if more than 1 side group on structure (7) (Total for Question 15 = 11 marks) (Total for Section B = 49 marks) Section C Question Number Acceptable Answers Additional Guidance Mark

An answer that makes refere nce to the following points:  reaction of benzene with Br and FeBr / AlBr (1)  reaction of bromobenzene with Mg (1)  in (dry) ether (1)  structure of Grignard reagent (1)  reaction of Grignard reagent with CO / (1)  (hydrolysis of acid salt) with dilute HCl (1) Alternative route for M5 and M6  reaction of Grignard reagent wi th HCHO (1)  (hydrolysis of acid salt) with dilute HCl and oxidation (of primary alcohol) with acidified Cr O 2- (1) See below for reaction scheme Ignore references to heating / refluxing throughout Allow Fe & Br Allow other halogens and halides Allow aluminium halide M5 and M6 with CO can be shown in the same part of the process providing it it’s clear that each step is separate e.g. by labelling (see example below) Allow any dilute acid / H + Ignore structure of salt Ignore H O (6) Allow CO and HCl as parts 1 and 2 of the final step if clearly labelled e.g. Question Number Acceptable Answers Additional Guidance Mark An answer that makes refere nce to the following points:  equation to show formation of electr ophile (1)  curly arrow from anywhere on the central ring to positive nitrogen (1)  structure of intermediate (1)  curly arrow from C-H bond to reform the ring (1)  equation showing regeneration of catalyst (1) HNO + H SO → NO + + HSO − + H O Or HNO + 2H SO → NO + + 2HSO − + H O + Or HNO + H SO → H NO + + HSO − and H NO + → NO + + H O Do not award H SO − in M1 but allow as potential TE in M5 Allow curly arrow from anywhere within the hexagon Horseshoe facing the tetrahedral carbon and covering at least three carbon atoms. Some part of the positive charge in the horseshoe Do not award dotted lines unless clearly part of a 3D structure Do not award M4 if substitution position is incorrect HSO − + H + → H SO Allow M5 as part of mechanism, with curly arrow from oxygen of HSO − to H on benzene ring (5) Example of mechanism for 16bi Ignore postion of ‘+’ in NO + No TE from incorrect species from equation

 calculation of molar mass of Pb(C H COO) (1)  calculation of amount of Pb(C H COO) (1)  deduction of amount of Ca(C H COO) .xH O (1)  calculation of M r of Ca(C H COO) .xH O (1)  calculation of mass of water in M r (1)  calculation of amount of water and hence x (1) Alternative route for M4-M6  calculation of mass of Ca(C H COO) (1)  calculation of mass of water in hydrated sample (1)  calculation of amount of water and hence x (1) Example of calculation 449.2 can be subsumed within M2 3.89 / 449.2 = 8.65984 x 10 − (mol) 1:1 so 8.65984 x 10 − (mol) M3 can be subsumed in M4 2.60 / 8.65984 x 10 − = 300.24 300.24 – (40.1 + (14x12) + (1x10) + (4x16) = 18.14 18.14 ÷ 18 = 1, so x = 1 Allow TE throughout Correct value for x with no working scores M6 only 8.65984 x 10 − x 282.1 = 2.44 g 2.60 – 2.44 = 0.16 g 0.16 ÷ 18 = 0.000889, so x = 1 Ignore SF except 1 SF for M1-M5 (6) Pearson Education Limited.
